From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

15th US national census

| Fifteenth census of the United States | |
| --- | --- |
| ---   ← [1920](/wiki/1920_United_States_census "1920 United States census")  **April 1, 1930**  [1940](/wiki/1940_United_States_census "1940 United States census") →   --- | |
| U.S. Census Bureau seal | |
| Population schedule  Indian census roll | |
| General information | |
| Country | United States |
| Results | |
| Total population | 122,775,046 (Increase 13.7%) |
| Most populous state | [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") 12,588,066 |
| Least populous state | [Nevada](/wiki/Nevada "Nevada") 91,058 |

The **1930 United States census**, conducted by the [Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau") one month from April 1, 1930, determined the resident population of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") to be 122,775,046, an increase of 13.7 percent over the 106,021,537 persons [enumerated](/wiki/Enumeration "Enumeration") during the [1920 census](/wiki/U.S._census,_1920 "U.S. census, 1920").

It was the first time the effects of [suburban sprawl](/wiki/Suburban_sprawl "Suburban sprawl") were recorded when, during the [Roaring Twenties](/wiki/Roaring_Twenties "Roaring Twenties"), a significant portion of the working population of [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") moved to [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), aided by the [George Washington Bridge](/wiki/George_Washington_Bridge "George Washington Bridge") which permitted quick transport from the state to [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan").

## Census questions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1930_United_States_census&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Census questions")]

The 1930 census collected the following information:[[1]](#cite_note-1)

* address
* name
* relationship to head of family
* home owned or rented
  + if owned, value of home
  + if rented, monthly rent
* whether owned a [radio set](/wiki/Radio_set "Radio set")
* whether on a farm
* sex
* race
* age
* marital status and, if married, age at first marriage
* school attendance
* literacy
* birthplace of person, and their parents
* if foreign born:
  + language spoken at home before coming to the U. S.
  + year of immigration
  + whether naturalized
  + ability to speak English
* occupation, industry and class of worker
* whether at work previous day (or last regular work day)
* veteran status
* if Indian:
  + whether of full or mixed blood
  + tribal affiliation

Full documentation for the 1930 census, including census forms and enumerator instructions, is available from the [Integrated Public Use Microdata Series](/wiki/Integrated_Public_Use_Microdata_Series "Integrated Public Use Microdata Series").

## Data availability

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1930_United_States_census&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Data availability")]

The original census enumeration sheets were [microfilmed](/wiki/Microform "Microform") by the Census Bureau in 1949, after which the original sheets were destroyed.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The microfilmed census is located on 2,667 rolls of microfilm, and available from the [National Archives and Records Administration](/wiki/National_Archives_and_Records_Administration "National Archives and Records Administration"). Several organizations also host images of the microfilmed census online, and digital indices.

[Microdata](/wiki/Microdata_(statistics) "Microdata (statistics)") from the 1930 census are freely available through the [Integrated Public Use Microdata Series](/wiki/Integrated_Public_Use_Microdata_Series "Integrated Public Use Microdata Series"). [Aggregate data](/wiki/Aggregate_data "Aggregate data") for small areas, together with electronic boundary files, can be downloaded from the [National Historical Geographic Information System](/wiki/National_Historical_Geographic_Information_System "National Historical Geographic Information System").
